content:
https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/issues/62

The reason glmark2 fails with "tyr: group_submit: invalid address space" when multiple groups share the same VM is:

  • When one of those groups is unbound, unbind_vm is called and vm.address_space is set to None.
  • When another group that shares the same VM later calls group_submit, sched.bind sees that the group is already bound and skips calling bind_group (and thus bind_vm). Later, when sched.submit runs, it reports "invalid address space" because the VM no longer has an address space assigned.

I added logic in sched.bind: when the group is already bound but the VM’s address space is None, I call bind_vm again. With this change, glmark2 runs for a while (I can see the spinning horse), then exits abnormally. If I run glmark2 again, ioctl(group_submit) returns EBUSY.

From debugging, it looks like the address space (AS) is being exhausted.

I looked at panthor’s implementation: it has a VM LRU reclaim path. Tyr appears to have something similar—VM reclaim depends on the group being considered idle—but mark_group_idle is never actually called, so VM reclaim never runs. I’m unsure how to fix this properly. @Daniel Almeida

I can't speak for the tyr-mini-demo branch, but this is a problem we knew we had for CSG slots (FW slots used to schedule GPU contexts, AKA groups). We're currently working on a generic solution to address the problem for both CSG and AS slots, but we're nowhere near to have this solution ported to the tyr-mini-demo branch, because there's a lot of other changes we've done along the way.

If you're interested, those changes are being discussed here https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/merge_requests/56

@Alvin Sun this is simply not implemented.

@Andrew Ballance you recently pushed some software scheduler changes, can you sync with Alvin on whether you have solved this particular problem? He is trying to fix issues with glmark2 in general

I had a similar problem when testing with the panthor_sched igt tests which also has multiple groups share a single vm. I fixed it by adding a refcount to each vm. the refcount is incremented in bind_vm and decremented in unbind_vm. the vm is only actually bound/unbound when the refcount is 0. This should fix the issue but I am unable to test glmark2 because I am currently away from my dev board.

Andrew Ballance said:

I had a similar problem when testing with the panthor_sched igt tests which also has multiple groups share a single vm. I fixed it by adding a refcount to each vm. the refcount is incremented in bind_vm and decremented in unbind_vm. the vm is only actually bound/unbound when the refcount is 0. This should fix the issue but I am unable to test glmark2 because I am currently away from my dev board.

Without address-space reclaim, adding reference counting to Vm has let glmark run further, and glmark2 can now be run repeatedly. A new problem has appeared: with texture-filter=mipmap, the drm_panthor_tiler_heap_create ioctl returns EBUSY. I’ll keep looking into the cause.

If you're interested, those changes are being discussed here https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/merge_requests/56

Thanks, I’ll go through those commits. One more thing: which branch should I work on—tyr-mini-demo or tyr-dev (the latter seems to be the latest)?

This should fix the issue but I am unable to test glmark2 because I am currently away from my dev board.

Without address-space reclaim, adding reference counting to Vm has let glmark run further, and glmark2 can now be run repeatedly. A new problem has appeared: with texture-filter=mipmap, the drm_panthor_tiler_heap_create ioctl returns EBUSY. I’ll keep looking into the cause.

[   85.388347] tyr: Binding kernel GEM object at VA range 0x80000f079000-0x80000f879000 (size: 0x800000) for VM 4
[   85.393472] tyr: mapping 8388608 bytes, iova: 0x80000f079000, prot 15
[   85.394783] tyr: Allocating heap chunk for context
[   85.395203] tyr: new_kernel_object: failed to allocate kernel range: EBUSY   // alloc_kernel_range return EBUSY

Following the panthor C driver’s VA layout, when the VA range is larger than 4GB the user and kernel regions are each set to full_va_range/2. The glmark mipmap issue is fixed, and I’m now trying to run the full glmark test.

One more thing: which branch should I work on—tyr-mini-demo or tyr-dev (the latter seems to be the latest)?

I would say, tyr-for-upstream even. Long story short, we've moved our focus towards upstreaming since last December. This means tyr-mini-demo stays what the branch says it is: a demo to show that having a rust kernel driver for Mali GPUs is possible, but it also means it's a dead end in the long run. Our upstreaming effort is not just about picking bits and pieces from this branch, it also involves

  • using generic abstractions provided by others (JobQueue to replace drm_sched, new version of GpuVm, ...)
  • redesigning the components when it makes sense to overcome the limitations/issues seen while working on the demo
  • revisiting the ownership model
  • revisiting the locking (fine grained locking as opposed to big locks protecting entire components)

We had a quick chat with @Daniel Almeida and @Deborah Brouwer yesterday, and we think it'd be unfair to have you and @Andrew Ballance work on things that will either be dropped entirely or massively revisited during the upstreaming process. Of course, if you're okay with that, we don't object to you working on this downstream branch. But if you're interested in participating in the upstreaming effort, we can probably find tasks for you to pick. This comes with the downside that you want be able to test things immediately, as you would with the demo branch, because there's quite a few missing pieces still. It's up to you, really. I just thought I'd mention the ongoing process so things are clear from the start and there's no disappointment down the road.

I know @Andrew Ballance started fixing Group scheduling in the demo/downstream branch. I can push the skeleton I started (based on @Deborah Brouwer branch) and we can discuss how we want to go from there, if you're interested. Note that it's very much just a stub implementation, with a bunch of TODOs, and quite a few unknowns. The goal is to mimic the group scheduling logic in Panthor (adjusted to Rust, of course), but there's a lot of work to get there.

@Alvin Sun I know that @Deborah Brouwer had a few ideas for things you could help us with. The plan is to have those tasks added to the Tyr workboard (select Tyr in the drop-down list, otherwise you'll see Panthor tasks) so people can claim the one they care about and let others know it's being worked on.

I can push the skeleton I started (based on @Deborah Brouwer branch) and we can discuss how we want to go from there, if you're interested.

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/bbrezillon/linux/-/commits/group-sched-wip

Thanks for the detailed introduction. I’d be happy to contribute to tyr development, both upstream and downstream.

It would help a lot if there were a concrete list of work items so I can pick tasks that match my skills. I have dedicated test hardware (RK3588S, NanoPi R6C), I’m comfortable with Linux/C, and I’m actively learning DRM and Rust.
I would be honoured to help you upstream the work that has already been done on tyr, and I think it would be very meaningful.
We need to do a bit of cleanup because I see a bunch of tasks that were created for the demo and are now done. We'll get back to you once this is done.
Okay. I’ll wait for your update.

That one could be a good fit https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/issues/11, but let's wait for @Deborah Brouwer to confirm, because I think she had a list of short/easy upstreaming tasks in mind.

Your approach is correct. After adding a refcount per VM and the IO VA space allocation similar to panthor/c, glmark now passes the full test run.

@Andrew Ballance You could consider submitting a refcount patch for tyr.

This is the refcount I implemented following your approach:

Assigned
Thanks!

While implementing the Tyr equivalent of panthor’s gpuvas debugfs interface, I ran into a question. In panthor, the code walks the rb.list list on struct drm_gpuvm to dump GPU VA info, but in Rust there’s no API to iterate over a C list_head. So I’m unsure whether to:

  1. Use the existing drm_gpuvm’s rb.list from Rust.
  2. Add a new field in Tyr’s GpuVmData (or similar) to track GPU VAs and iterate over that from Rust.

Which of these is a better fit, or is there another approach you’d recommend?
The GpuVm abstractions could have an interface to iterate gpuva entries.
Should we implement it by iterating over drm_gpuvm’s rb.list?
Sure, that seems like a good implementation.
Note that this touches the interval tree, so only the GpuVmCore has access to it.
So it must be a method on the core.
Okay. Thanks!

Boris Brezillon said:

That one could be a good fit https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/issues/11, but let's wait for Deborah Brouwer to confirm, because I think she had a list of short/easy upstreaming tasks in mind.

I've implemented the gpuvas output based on the group-sched-wip branch you shared.
The output looks like this:

root@nanopi-r6s:/sys/kernel/debug/tyr-card0# cat gpuvas
DRM GPU VA space ("Tyr::GpuVm") [0x0000000000000000;0x0001000000000000]
Kernel reserved node [0x0000000000000000;0x0000000000000000]
 VAs | start              | range              | end                | object             | object offset
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     | 0x0000000000000000 | 0x0000000000001000 | 0x0000000000001000 | 0xffff00010c5e9c00 | 0x0000000000000000
     | 0x0000000000400000 | 0x0000000000001000 | 0x0000000000401000 | 0xffff00010c5e9400 | 0x0000000000000000
     | 0x0000000000401000 | 0x0000000000002000 | 0x0000000000403000 | 0xffff00010c5e9800 | 0x0000000000000000
     | 0x0000000000800000 | 0x0000000000020000 | 0x0000000000820000 | 0xffff00010c5ea000 | 0x0000000000000000
     | 0x0000000001000000 | 0x0000000000040000 | 0x0000000001040000 | 0xffff00010c5ea800 | 0x0000000000000000
     | 0x0000000002000000 | 0x0000000000040000 | 0x0000000002040000 | 0xffff00010c5ea400 | 0x0000000000000000
     | 0x0000000004000000 | 0x000000000000c000 | 0x000000000400c000 | 0xffff00010c5eac00 | 0x0000000000000000

The tyr-for-upstream branch and the upstream code do not contain tyr/vm.rs or any gpuvm-related code, so it doesn’t seem possible to send the code through the mailing list. How can I share my code for review?
Should I create an MR at https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux ? When I tried to fork Panfrost/linux, I got "You cannot create projects in your personal namespace".
Is 0xffff00010c5e9c00 a kernel address? I'm not sure we want to expose kernel addresses to userspace. It can be an ASLR bypass.
Yes. That’s a kernel address, and we can change it later to output the hash produced by "%p" instead.

Two more tasks to look at that should be relatively easy, in case anyone is interested

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/issues/68
https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/panfrost/linux/-/issues/69
I can take issue 68 first. If nobody else wants them, you can assign both issues/68 and issues/69 to me—I’m already working on bo- and vm-related debugfs interfaces.
